@raspbeguy 

> I just express my feeling about what I objectively think to be the good way to progress, 

Expressing your opinions is always encouraged here.  And both you and @lasers raise some good points.  There are definitely some things that need improving with the project.  Some documentation is desperately needed such as the formatting and some better guidance on module development.

From my point of view I want things to be easy for user and I think we should develop for 2 distinct users.

a) Users who just want things to work.  To me a good example would be the `volume_status` as a user I should be able to just do `order += 'volume_status'` and should get a sensible working volume control (I don't think we are here as the volume changing controls isn't by default)

b) expert users who want to set things up exactly as they want full control of colors etc.  This also to an extent includes people who just want to make small changes say to the format.

Because of this I want to keep things like default formats really simple.  @lasers for example likes to make lots of format usage (different colors for different states)  I resist all attempts for complex default formats (@ultrabug is more forgiving here)

for me simplicity is key so I will always struggle with things like

```
-    format = '{caps} {num} {scr}'

+    format = ('[\?if=caps_lock&color=good CAPS|\?color=bad CAPS] '
+              '[\?if=num_lock&color=good NUM|\?color=bad NUM] '
+              '[\?if=scroll_lock&color=good SCR|\?color=bad SCR]')
```

mainly because to me it shows we need to improve the formatter to make this simpler

I'm also keen to have as few modules as we can that do the same thing (music players being an example)  I'd much prefer one really solid mpris module rather than many different modules one for each player.

@lasers 
>  I make many PRs. It scares and/or annoys them.

I'd say that it is more it makes it harder to manage things as reviewing all open PRs is not easily possible when there are so many of them.  Things can get lost etc.  Sometimes things are just slow.  You also like to make many changes in a PR and so a small part can easily block the entire PR.  I know this is frustrating for you.

As an aside, the only reason botocore's data files are called `_endpoints` and `_retry` are because back in the day, botocore didn't track service models by api version.  They were all in a single, top level directory by name, e.g: `['ec2.json', 's3.json', 'elb.json', ...]`.  Therefore, putting an `endpoints.json` in this same directory would make botocore think there's an "endpoints" service.  Even if the botocore loaders were hard coded to ignore `endpoints.json` from the `listdir()` call, we'd still be in trouble if an actual service called "endpoints" ever came out.  To workaround this, I just prefixed all non service model files with `_` and had the loaders ignore anything starting with `_`, giving us `_endpoints.json` and `_retry.json`.  This is no longer necessary now that each service is in its own directory, but we never went back and removed the leading `_` because there wasn't a real pressing reason to do so.  I never meant it as any kind of statement of internal/implementation detail.

Because the span starts and ends are inclusive, your `span_widths` calculation above is actually off by one, and you compensate for that here with a `<=` instead of a `<`.  The fact that `raw_span_indices` actually gives the indices _backwards_ also confused me a bit.  There was a lot going on in these few lines... =).

I'm not sure what the best thing to do here is, but at the least some more detailed comments would be nice, because this is a potentially pretty confusing block of code.  E.g., if you want to keep the off-by-one issue in `span_widths`, that's fine, as long as there's a note describing this.  Might also be worth stating (even with an example) that `raw_span_indices` will be backwards, so if you're doing a computation that depends on order, you might be surprised.

I don't think this is problematic.

- There is a small, finite number of existing APIs that have handwritten client libraries, for which this flag needs to be set by hand.
- The cost of creating new handwritten client libraries is very high compared to the cost of manually setting this flag.
- There will always be some configuration required for each API we generate.
- If there are values that are always deterministically knowable from the input, they should be set in the code rather than through configuration.

(That's not to say, of course, that we shouldn't make an effort to minimize the amount of configuration work necessary.)

This is counting the total number of pending transaction across all channels, which is not what we want, we want to limit the number of transactions on a per channel basis.

It's better to use the constant to filter outgoing transfer in these places (here we want to avoid sending transfers which would be reject by the node's partners):

- [initiator.py:next_channel_from_routes](https://github.com/raiden-network/raiden/blob/b3251cb92fe7eb6a39649a08e3b500b0c33fc88d/raiden/transfer/mediated_transfer/initiator.py#L51-L80)
- [mediator.py:next_channel_from_routes](https://github.com/raiden-network/raiden/blob/b3251cb92fe7eb6a39649a08e3b500b0c33fc88d/raiden/transfer/mediated_transfer/mediator.py#L282-L328)

And when receiving a transfer at the channel level (here we want to avoid a balance proof which contains a large number of pending transfer, which the node cannot unlock on-chain because of the gas limitations):

- [channel.py:valid_lockedtransfer_check](https://github.com/raiden-network/raiden/blob/b3251cb92fe7eb6a39649a08e3b500b0c33fc88d/raiden/transfer/channel.py#L389)

I'm not sure if this is the right solution because it would put the extras after specifiers, markers, and hashes as formatted by `as_line()`. The better solution is probably to have `as_line()` take an optional `force_extras` parameter:

```python
    def as_line(self, sources=None, *, force_extras=False):
        line = "{0}{1}{2}{3}{4}".format(
            self.req.line_part,
            self.extras_as_pip if force_extras or not self.is_vcs else "",
            self.specifiers if self.specifiers else "",
            self.markers_as_pip,
            self.hashes_as_pip,
        )
```

What do you think?
